texts.orig: Flexible Language Modeling in Continuous Space with Transformer-based Autoregressive Flows

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Abstract
texts.text: Abstract

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Autoregressive models have driven remarkable progress in language modeling. Their foundational reliance on discrete tokens, unidirectional context, and singlepass decoding, while central to their success, also inspires the exploration of a design space that could offer new axes of modeling flexibility. In this work, we explore an alternative paradigm, shifting language modeling from a discrete token space to a continuous latent space. We propose a novel framework TarFlowLM , that employs transformer-based autoregressive normalizing flows [73] to model these continuous representations. This approach unlocks substantial flexibility, enabling the construction of models that can capture global bi-directional context through stacked, alternating-direction autoregressive transformations, support block-wise generation with flexible token patch sizes, and facilitate a hierarchical multi-pass generation process. We further propose new mixture-based coupling transformations designed to capture complex dependencies within the latent space shaped by discrete data, and demonstrate theoretical connections to conventional discrete autoregressive models. Extensive experiments on language modeling benchmarks demonstrate strong likelihood performance and highlight the flexible modeling capabilities inherent in our framework.
texts.text: Autoregressive models have driven remarkable progress in language modeling. Their foundational reliance on discrete tokens, unidirectional context, and singlepass decoding, while central to their success, also inspires the exploration of a design space that could offer new axes of modeling flexibility. In this work, we explore an alternative paradigm, shifting language modeling from a discrete token space to a continuous latent space. We propose a novel framework TarFlowLM , that employs transformer-based autoregressive normalizing flows [73] to model these continuous representations. This approach unlocks substantial flexibility, enabling the construction of models that can capture global bi-directional context through stacked, alternating-direction autoregressive transformations, support block-wise generation with flexible token patch sizes, and facilitate a hierarchical multi-pass generation process. We further propose new mixture-based coupling transformations designed to capture complex dependencies within the latent space shaped by discrete data, and demonstrate theoretical connections to conventional discrete autoregressive models. Extensive experiments on language modeling benchmarks demonstrate strong likelihood performance and highlight the flexible modeling capabilities inherent in our framework.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 1 Introduction
texts.text: 1 Introduction

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Transformer-based autoregressive models [5, 66] have emerged as the dominant paradigm for language modeling, achieving remarkable performance by predicting discrete tokens one at a time under the cross-entropy objective. By scaling both model size and training data, these models excel at next-token prediction and have become the foundation for modern natural language generation systems.
texts.text: Transformer-based autoregressive models [5, 66] have emerged as the dominant paradigm for language modeling, achieving remarkable performance by predicting discrete tokens one at a time under the cross-entropy objective. By scaling both model size and training data, these models excel at next-token prediction and have become the foundation for modern natural language generation systems.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Their remarkable success provides a strong foundation and inspires further exploration into the design space of autoregressive sequence modelling . While the established paradigm of discrete, typically unidirectional autoregressive generation offers a powerful and well-understood framework, we consider whether alternative formulations might offer new dimensions of flexibility and open different avenues for model construction. This work investigates such a possibility: What if autoregressive language modeling were reformulated within a continuous latent space? Moving to continuous representations allows for the preservation of the sequential factorization familiar from autoregressive methods, while potentially unlocking new modeling capabilities.
texts.text: Their remarkable success provides a strong foundation and inspires further exploration into the design space of autoregressive sequence modelling . While the established paradigm of discrete, typically unidirectional autoregressive generation offers a powerful and well-understood framework, we consider whether alternative formulations might offer new dimensions of flexibility and open different avenues for model construction. This work investigates such a possibility: What if autoregressive language modeling were reformulated within a continuous latent space? Moving to continuous representations allows for the preservation of the sequential factorization familiar from autoregressive methods, while potentially unlocking new modeling capabilities.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Building on this perspective, we propose a novel framework that employs Transformer-based autoregressive normalizing flows [73] to model these continuous latent representations. This shift from discrete token space to a continuous latent space is key to unlocking substantial modeling flexibility. Our formulation facilitates the construction of models capable of capturing global bi-directional context through stacked, alternating-direction autoregressive transformations. It also supports blockwise generation with adaptable token patch sizes and enables a hierarchical multi-pass generation process, allowing for the observation and influence of sequence formation through intermediate stages. These capabilities arise naturally from the continuous and invertible nature of the flow-based transformations.
texts.text: Building on this perspective, we propose a novel framework that employs Transformer-based autoregressive normalizing flows [73] to model these continuous latent representations. This shift from discrete token space to a continuous latent space is key to unlocking substantial modeling flexibility. Our formulation facilitates the construction of models capable of capturing global bi-directional context through stacked, alternating-direction autoregressive transformations. It also supports blockwise generation with adaptable token patch sizes and enables a hierarchical multi-pass generation process, allowing for the observation and influence of sequence formation through intermediate stages. These capabilities arise naturally from the continuous and invertible nature of the flow-based transformations.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: To this end, we present TarFlowLM , a Transformer-based Auto-Regressive Flow Language Model that uses mixture-based coupling transformations to effectively model the complex, multi-modal distributions created when mapping discrete data to a continuous latent space. Our key theoretical contribution is establishing the equivalence that transforms these mixture distributions into exact, invertible normalizing flow layers. Specifically, we show that a single-dimensional Mixture of Gaussians (MoG) distribution can be realized as a 1D Mixture-CDF flow, and a multi-dimensional MoG distribution as a Mixture-Rosenblatt flow. We also draw theoretical links between our continuous approach and standard discrete autoregressive models. Experiments on language modeling benchmarks show that our method achieves strong likelihood performance and, importantly, enables greater modeling flexibility. Our work expands the possibilities of language modeling by extending autoregressive methods into the continuous domain.
texts.text: To this end, we present TarFlowLM , a Transformer-based Auto-Regressive Flow Language Model that uses mixture-based coupling transformations to effectively model the complex, multi-modal distributions created when mapping discrete data to a continuous latent space. Our key theoretical contribution is establishing the equivalence that transforms these mixture distributions into exact, invertible normalizing flow layers. Specifically, we show that a single-dimensional Mixture of Gaussians (MoG) distribution can be realized as a 1D Mixture-CDF flow, and a multi-dimensional MoG distribution as a Mixture-Rosenblatt flow. We also draw theoretical links between our continuous approach and standard discrete autoregressive models. Experiments on language modeling benchmarks show that our method achieves strong likelihood performance and, importantly, enables greater modeling flexibility. Our work expands the possibilities of language modeling by extending autoregressive methods into the continuous domain.

texts.orig: Normalizing flows offer a method for constructing flexible probability distributions over continuous variables [51]. The core idea is to start with a simple base distribution p base ( u ) defined on R d , often a standard Gaussian, and transform samples u ∼ p base ( u ) using an invertible and differentiable function f : R d → R d , known as a diffeomorphism. This process yields a variable z = f -1 ( u ) that follows a potentially much more complex distribution p ( z ) . A key advantage of this approach is that the probability density function p ( z ) can be computed exactly using the change of variables formula:
texts.text: Normalizing flows offer a method for constructing flexible probability distributions over continuous variables [51]. The core idea is to start with a simple base distribution p base ( u ) defined on R d , often a standard Gaussian, and transform samples u ∼ p base ( u ) using an invertible and differentiable function f : R d → R d , known as a diffeomorphism. This process yields a variable z = f -1 ( u ) that follows a potentially much more complex distribution p ( z ) . A key advantage of this approach is that the probability density function p ( z ) can be computed exactly using the change of variables formula:

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: log p ( z ) = log p base ( f ( z )) + log | det J f ( z ) | (1)

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Here, J f ( z ) is the Jacobian matrix of the transformation f evaluated at z , and | det( · ) | denotes the absolute value of the determinant. Complex distributions are typically modeled by composing multiple simple transformations f = f ( L ) ◦ · · · ◦ f (1) , where each layer f ( ℓ ) is designed to be easily invertible and have a tractable Jacobian determinant. The log-density then becomes a sum of log-determinant terms from each layer, plus the log-density of the final transformed variable under the base distribution:
texts.text: Here, J f ( z ) is the Jacobian matrix of the transformation f evaluated at z , and | det( · ) | denotes the absolute value of the determinant. Complex distributions are typically modeled by composing multiple simple transformations f = f ( L ) ◦ · · · ◦ f (1) , where each layer f ( ℓ ) is designed to be easily invertible and have a tractable Jacobian determinant. The log-density then becomes a sum of log-determinant terms from each layer, plus the log-density of the final transformed variable under the base distribution:

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The main challenge in designing normalizing flows lies in choosing transformations f ( ℓ ) that are both expressive enough to model complex data distributions and computationally efficient, particularly regarding the calculation of the inverse f -1 and the Jacobian determinant det J f .
texts.text: The main challenge in designing normalizing flows lies in choosing transformations f ( ℓ ) that are both expressive enough to model complex data distributions and computationally efficient, particularly regarding the calculation of the inverse f -1 and the Jacobian determinant det J f .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Aprominent and widely used class is autoregressive flows [39, 50]. These structure the transformation f such that u i depends on z j with j < i . This ensures the Jacobian J f is triangular, making its determinant the product of diagonal entries, computable in O ( d ) time. A common choice for the element-wise transformation is the affine function: z i = α i u i + β i . In the autoregressive setting, the scale α i > 0 and shift β i for dimension i are functions of the preceding dimensions, e.g.,
texts.text: Aprominent and widely used class is autoregressive flows [39, 50]. These structure the transformation f such that u i depends on z j with j < i . This ensures the Jacobian J f is triangular, making its determinant the product of diagonal entries, computable in O ( d ) time. A common choice for the element-wise transformation is the affine function: z i = α i u i + β i . In the autoregressive setting, the scale α i > 0 and shift β i for dimension i are functions of the preceding dimensions, e.g.,

texts.label: caption
texts.orig: Figure 1: Our approach transforms discrete token sequences into a continuous latent space modeled by stacked Transformer-based autoregressive normalizing flows, creating a fully invertible pipeline for training and generation. Left: During training (Red top-down arrow), tokens are encoded into Gaussian latents by a VAE encoder, transformed through flow layers into standard Gaussian variables, and reconstructed by a tied decoder. At inference (Blue bottom-up arrow), samples from a standard Gaussian are inverted through the flows and decoded into text. Right: Key flexibility features: (a) Tokens can be grouped into patches and embedded into latent space using mixtureof-Gaussians transformations with dynamic vocabulary sizes; (b) Block-wise autoregressive flows alternate directions, enabling context exchange between initially independent tokens; (c) Channel mixing permutes latent dimensions between flows to facilitate cross-token information sharing.
texts.text: Figure 1: Our approach transforms discrete token sequences into a continuous latent space modeled by stacked Transformer-based autoregressive normalizing flows, creating a fully invertible pipeline for training and generation. Left: During training (Red top-down arrow), tokens are encoded into Gaussian latents by a VAE encoder, transformed through flow layers into standard Gaussian variables, and reconstructed by a tied decoder. At inference (Blue bottom-up arrow), samples from a standard Gaussian are inverted through the flows and decoded into text. Right: Key flexibility features: (a) Tokens can be grouped into patches and embedded into latent space using mixtureof-Gaussians transformations with dynamic vocabulary sizes; (b) Block-wise autoregressive flows alternate directions, enabling context exchange between initially independent tokens; (c) Channel mixing permutes latent dimensions between flows to facilitate cross-token information sharing.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3 Autoregressive Language Modeling in Continuous Latent Space
texts.text: 3 Autoregressive Language Modeling in Continuous Latent Space

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Notation. We use italics for scalars ( x t ), bold for vectors ( z t ), and bold with indices for sequences ( x 1: T , z 1: T ).
texts.text: Notation. We use italics for scalars ( x t ), bold for vectors ( z t ), and bold with indices for sequences ( x 1: T , z 1: T ).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We propose utilizing the Variational Autoencoder (VAE) framework [38]. The core idea is to map sequences of discrete tokens x 1: T = ( x 1 , . . . , x T ) into sequences of continuous latent variables z 1: T = ( z 1 , . . . , z T ) , where each z t ∈ R d . We then model the joint distribution p ( z 1: T ) , the prior, to capture sequential dependencies. This approach aims to preserve the sequential factorization structure familiar from autoregressive models while gaining the benefits of end-to-end differentiability inherent in continuous spaces.
texts.text: We propose utilizing the Variational Autoencoder (VAE) framework [38]. The core idea is to map sequences of discrete tokens x 1: T = ( x 1 , . . . , x T ) into sequences of continuous latent variables z 1: T = ( z 1 , . . . , z T ) , where each z t ∈ R d . We then model the joint distribution p ( z 1: T ) , the prior, to capture sequential dependencies. This approach aims to preserve the sequential factorization structure familiar from autoregressive models while gaining the benefits of end-to-end differentiability inherent in continuous spaces.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3.1 The Bridge: Encoder and Decoder
texts.text: 3.1 The Bridge: Encoder and Decoder

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The encoder and decoder facilitate the transition between the discrete data space of tokens and the continuous latent space of vectors z t .
texts.text: The encoder and decoder facilitate the transition between the discrete data space of tokens and the continuous latent space of vectors z t .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Encoder q ( z 1: T | x 1: T ) . We use a factorized encoder, q ( z 1: T | x 1: T ) = ∏ T t =1 q ( z t | x t ) , where each token k ∈ { 1 , . . . , V } is assigned a learnable mean µ k ∈ R d and variance σ 2 k for an isotropic Gaussian: q ( z t | x t = k ) = N ( z t ; µ k , σ 2 k I ) ≡ N k ( z t ) . Thus, the codebook { µ k , σ 2 k } V k =1 maps each token to a Gaussian distribution in latent space.
texts.text: Encoder q ( z 1: T | x 1: T ) . We use a factorized encoder, q ( z 1: T | x 1: T ) = ∏ T t =1 q ( z t | x t ) , where each token k ∈ { 1 , . . . , V } is assigned a learnable mean µ k ∈ R d and variance σ 2 k for an isotropic Gaussian: q ( z t | x t = k ) = N ( z t ; µ k , σ 2 k I ) ≡ N k ( z t ) . Thus, the codebook { µ k , σ 2 k } V k =1 maps each token to a Gaussian distribution in latent space.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Decoder p ( x 1: T | z 1: T ) . The decoder maps each latent vector z t back to a distribution over tokens, with again a factorized structure p ( x 1: T | z 1: T ) = ∏ T t =1 p ( x t | z t ) . We reuse the encoder 's Gaussian parameters { µ k , σ 2 k } V k =1 for the decoder with a Bayesian posterior parameterization assuming an uniform prior: p ( x t = k | z t ) = p ( x t = k ) q ( z t | x t = k ) ∑ V j =1 p ( x t = j ) q ( z t | x t = j ) = N k ( z t ) ∑ V j =1 N j ( z t ) . In other words, the probability of decoding token k from z t is determined by how likely z t is under the k -th encoder Gaussian, compared to all other tokens.
texts.text: Decoder p ( x 1: T | z 1: T ) . The decoder maps each latent vector z t back to a distribution over tokens, with again a factorized structure p ( x 1: T | z 1: T ) = ∏ T t =1 p ( x t | z t ) . We reuse the encoder 's Gaussian parameters { µ k , σ 2 k } V k =1 for the decoder with a Bayesian posterior parameterization assuming an uniform prior: p ( x t = k | z t ) = p ( x t = k ) q ( z t | x t = k ) ∑ V j =1 p ( x t = j ) q ( z t | x t = j ) = N k ( z t ) ∑ V j =1 N j ( z t ) . In other words, the probability of decoding token k from z t is determined by how likely z t is under the k -th encoder Gaussian, compared to all other tokens.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3.2 Autoregressive Prior Modeling for p ( z 1: T )
texts.text: 3.2 Autoregressive Prior Modeling for p ( z 1: T )

texts.label: text
texts.orig: To model the prior p ( z 1: T ) over latent sequences, we use the standard autoregressive factorization: p ( z 1: T ) = ∏ T t =1 p ( z t | z <t ) , where z <t = ( z 1 , . . . , z t -1 ) is the history. The main task is to define the conditional distribution p ( z t | z <t ) . We present two forms of parameterizing the conditional p ( z t | z <t ) , one with dimension-wise autoregressive factorization Sec. 3.2.1, the other with token-wise autoregressive factorization Sec. 3.2.2. In each formulation, we use a mixture-based probability distribution, and show that we can equivalently convert the mixture probability density into an equivalent invertible normalizing flow layer. Finally, we show how stacking such flow layers yields an expressive and flexible prior model p ( z 1: T ) .
texts.text: To model the prior p ( z 1: T ) over latent sequences, we use the standard autoregressive factorization: p ( z 1: T ) = ∏ T t =1 p ( z t | z <t ) , where z <t = ( z 1 , . . . , z t -1 ) is the history. The main task is to define the conditional distribution p ( z t | z <t ) . We present two forms of parameterizing the conditional p ( z t | z <t ) , one with dimension-wise autoregressive factorization Sec. 3.2.1, the other with token-wise autoregressive factorization Sec. 3.2.2. In each formulation, we use a mixture-based probability distribution, and show that we can equivalently convert the mixture probability density into an equivalent invertible normalizing flow layer. Finally, we show how stacking such flow layers yields an expressive and flexible prior model p ( z 1: T ) .

texts.orig: Transforming 1 -D Mixture Density to an Invertible Flow. The conditional 1D mixture density p mix1 ( · ) from Eq. (5) naturally defines an invertible normalizing flow layer. This layer transforms an input z t,i into u t,i , such that u t,i follows a standard normal distribution N (0 , 1) . The transformation is given by u t,i = Φ -1 ( F mix1 ( z t,i ; z <t , z t,<i )) , where F mix1 is the CDF of p mix1 and Φ -1 is the inverse standard normal CDF. This mapping is invertible and its key properties are summarized in Table 1 (see Appendix A for details).
texts.text: Transforming 1 -D Mixture Density to an Invertible Flow. The conditional 1D mixture density p mix1 ( · ) from Eq. (5) naturally defines an invertible normalizing flow layer. This layer transforms an input z t,i into u t,i , such that u t,i follows a standard normal distribution N (0 , 1) . The transformation is given by u t,i = Φ -1 ( F mix1 ( z t,i ; z <t , z t,<i )) , where F mix1 is the CDF of p mix1 and Φ -1 is the inverse standard normal CDF. This mapping is invertible and its key properties are summarized in Table 1 (see Appendix A for details).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Transforming d -D Mixture Density to an Invertible Flow. The d -dimensional conditional mixture density p mixd can also be realized as an invertible normalizing flow layer. Unlike the 1D case, where the CDF and its inverse are available in closed form, a direct CDF-based transformation is not tractable for general d -dimensional mixtures. Instead, we use a sequential transformation u = g d ( z ; z <t ) based on Rosenblatt's theorem [55], which maps z ∈ R d to u ∈ R d such that u follows a standard Gaussian N ( 0 , I d ) . This process, described in Algorithm 1, proceeds dimension by dimension: at each step i , a 1D Mixture-CDF transform is applied to z i , conditioned on the previous components z <i . The full derivation, including the inverse transformation, is given in Appendix B.
texts.text: Transforming d -D Mixture Density to an Invertible Flow. The d -dimensional conditional mixture density p mixd can also be realized as an invertible normalizing flow layer. Unlike the 1D case, where the CDF and its inverse are available in closed form, a direct CDF-based transformation is not tractable for general d -dimensional mixtures. Instead, we use a sequential transformation u = g d ( z ; z <t ) based on Rosenblatt's theorem [55], which maps z ∈ R d to u ∈ R d such that u follows a standard Gaussian N ( 0 , I d ) . This process, described in Algorithm 1, proceeds dimension by dimension: at each step i , a 1D Mixture-CDF transform is applied to z i , conditioned on the previous components z <i . The full derivation, including the inverse transformation, is given in Appendix B.

texts.label: text

This relationship, also summarized in Table 1, shows that the log-probability of z under the d -dimensional mixture is given by the change of variables formula:

This relationship, also summarized in Table 1, shows that the log-probability of z under the d -dimensional mixture is given by the change of variables formula:

texts.text: This stacked structure enables the model to incrementally transform the latent variables and capture complex dependencies. Different flow types and autoregressive directions can be combined across layers for greater expressiveness.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Progressive Layer-wise Training Our stacked normalizing flow prior naturally supports progressive layer-wise training, which simplifies optimization for deep flow models. We start by training the first block of flow layers (e.g., f (1) to f ( k 1 ) ), possibly together with the V AE encoder and decoder, until their ELBO contribution (Eq. equation 9) stabilizes on a validation set. Once converged, we freeze their parameters and add the next block of layers (e.g., f ( k 1 +1) to f ( k 2 ) ), training only the new layers to further transform the fixed output from the previous block. This process repeats, adding and training new layers while keeping earlier ones fixed, until all L flow layers are trained. Progressive training improves stability, as each new layer incrementally normalizes the representation, and helps the model reach better optima in deep architectures.
texts.text: Progressive Layer-wise Training Our stacked normalizing flow prior naturally supports progressive layer-wise training, which simplifies optimization for deep flow models. We start by training the first block of flow layers (e.g., f (1) to f ( k 1 ) ), possibly together with the V AE encoder and decoder, until their ELBO contribution (Eq. equation 9) stabilizes on a validation set. Once converged, we freeze their parameters and add the next block of layers (e.g., f ( k 1 +1) to f ( k 2 ) ), training only the new layers to further transform the fixed output from the previous block. This process repeats, adding and training new layers while keeping earlier ones fixed, until all L flow layers are trained. Progressive training improves stability, as each new layer incrementally normalizes the representation, and helps the model reach better optima in deep architectures.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4 Experiments
texts.text: 4 Experiments

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We conduct experiments to evaluate our proposed continuous latent space framework for autoregressive language modeling. The primary objectives are twofold: first, to assess its likelihood performance on standard language modeling benchmarks, and second, to demonstrate the flexible modeling capabilities enabled by the continuous and flow-based formulation.
texts.text: We conduct experiments to evaluate our proposed continuous latent space framework for autoregressive language modeling. The primary objectives are twofold: first, to assess its likelihood performance on standard language modeling benchmarks, and second, to demonstrate the flexible modeling capabilities enabled by the continuous and flow-based formulation.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Datasets and Evaluation. We evaluate our models on standard language modeling benchmarks, specifically TEXT8 [47] and OPENWEBTEXT [22]. For character-level tasks, we report bits per character (BPC), while for word-level tasks, we use perplexity (PPL) or negative ELBO (NELBO) on the respective test sets, given the V AE-based nature of our model.
texts.text: Datasets and Evaluation. We evaluate our models on standard language modeling benchmarks, specifically TEXT8 [47] and OPENWEBTEXT [22]. For character-level tasks, we report bits per character (BPC), while for word-level tasks, we use perplexity (PPL) or negative ELBO (NELBO) on the respective test sets, given the V AE-based nature of our model.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Model Configurations. We conduct our experiments using the GPT2-Small architecture, following the setup in [1, 56, 58]. This model has 12 layers, a hidden size of 768, and 12 attention heads. All conditional distributions within the flow layers are parameterized by Transformers, as described in Sections 3.2.1 and 3.2.2. We primarily explore two model configurations:
texts.text: Model Configurations. We conduct our experiments using the GPT2-Small architecture, following the setup in [1, 56, 58]. This model has 12 layers, a hidden size of 768, and 12 attention heads. All conditional distributions within the flow layers are parameterized by Transformers, as described in Sections 3.2.1 and 3.2.2. We primarily explore two model configurations:

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Mix-1 : This variant employs a dimension-wise autoregressive 1-D mixture prior ( p ( z t,i | z <t , z t,<i ) ; see Sec. 3.2.1). The model consists of three flow layers with alternating directions (left-to-right and right-to-left), starting from the z → u direction. The number of Transformer layers in each flow is [2 , 2 , 8] . Unless otherwise specified in ablation studies, we use V = 64 mixture components for OPENWEBTEXT and V = 27 for TEXT8. To achieve dimension-wise autoregression, we use the same MLP-based MAF [50] in [41].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Perplexity evaluation We assess the language modeling capabilities of our approach by training models on three standard benchmarks: TEXT8, and OPENWEBTEXT. For each dataset, we evaluate the NELBO of our trained models on their respective validation or test splits. Specifically, for TEXT8, we follow the established character-level setup and data splits, typically using fixed-length text chunks for training. For OPENWEBTEXT, we train models using the common GPT-2 tokenization and a context length of 1,024 tokens, reserving a portion of the dataset for validation, upon which NELBO is reported. We report results in Table 2.

For each dataset, we evaluate the NELBO of our trained models on their respective validation or test splits. Specifically, for TEXT8, we follow the established character-level setup and data splits, typically using fixed-length text chunks for training. For OPENWEBTEXT, we train models using the common GPT-2 tokenization and a context length of 1,024 tokens, reserving a portion of the dataset for validation, upon which NELBO is reported. We report results in Table 2.

texts.orig: Results discussion. Table 2 shows the discrete autoregressive Transformer is best (Text8 1.23 BPC; OWT 17.54 PPL). TarFlowLM is competitive among continuous-space/flow baselines: Mix-d (22.64 PPL; 1.30 BPC) outperforms several diffusion/NF methods and approaches the strongest diffusion results; Mix-1 is on par with Gaussian diffusion. While below discrete AR on PPL/BPC, TarFlowLM adds bi-directional, block-wise, and hierarchical flexibility.

TarFlowLM is competitive among continuous-space/flow baselines: Mix-d (22.64 PPL; 1.30 BPC) outperforms several diffusion/NF methods and approaches the strongest diffusion results; Mix-1 is on par with Gaussian diffusion. While below discrete AR on PPL/BPC, TarFlowLM adds bi-directional, block-wise, and hierarchical flexibility.

texts.orig: Importance of Mixture Coupling We provide empirical evidence for the effectiveness of the mixture coupling layers introduced earlier. Specifically, we compare two model variants on the OPENWEBTEXT dataset: (1) a baseline using only affine coupling layers (6 flow blocks, each with a 2-layer causal transformer, totaling 12 transformer layers), and (2) an identical architecture but with Mix-1D (mixture) coupling layers. As shown in Fig. 3, incorporating mixture couplings leads to a substantial improvement in negative ELBO, clearly demonstrating their importance for modeling discrete text data.
texts.text: Importance of Mixture Coupling We provide empirical evidence for the effectiveness of the mixture coupling layers introduced earlier. As shown in Fig. 3, incorporating mixture couplings leads to a substantial improvement in negative ELBO, clearly demonstrating their importance for modeling discrete text data.

texts.orig: Flexible Patch Size: Block-wise Multi-token Generation Our framework offers greater flexibility in sequence modeling by moving beyond the traditional single-token generation approach. Instead, it processes "patches" of multiple tokens at once, allowing for simultaneous prediction and generation. In contrast, standard discrete autoregressive language models-even those trained with multi-token objectives [21, 42]-still generate tokens strictly one at a time to maintain autoregressive consistency, since generating multiple tokens together breaks their core assumptions. We address this by using a continuous latent space and stacked normalizing flows to capture dependencies within each token patch. First, stacked autoregressive flows with alternating directions (see Fig. 1, patch size two) enable later layers to propagate and refine joint information, even if earlier blocks treat tokens as conditionally independent (as shown by the shaded area). Second, intra-patch dependencies are further modeled by mixing or permuting latent embedding dimensions between flow layers (channel mixing, Algorithm 2), allowing information to flow between token positions within a patch. Together, these mechanisms let the model effectively learn intra-patch dependencies. Block-wise generation also changes the computational landscape: it shifts some of the workload from sequence length to model depth (number of flow layers), reducing the effective sequence length. As shown in Fig. 7, this can significantly lower the total FLOPs needed to process a sequence (e.g., 1024 tokens) compared to standard transformers, especially with larger patch sizes, all within a generalized autoregressive framework. We also trained a discrete autoregressive language model with a two-token prediction objective and compared its generative perplexity (measured by GPT-2 Large) to our model, conditioning on varying prefix lengths. The results show a large gap, highlighting how standard AR LMs break down when the next-single-token assumption is violated. See Fig. 5 for generative PPL with patch size 2. See Table 3 for unconditional samples from TarFlowLM .

We address this by using a continuous latent space and stacked normalizing flows to capture dependencies within each token patch. First, stacked autoregressive flows with alternating directions (see Fig. 1, patch size two) enable later layers to propagate and refine joint information, even if earlier blocks treat tokens as conditionally independent (as shown by the shaded area). Second, intra-patch dependencies are further modeled by mixing or permuting latent embedding dimensions between flow layers (channel mixing, Algorithm 2), allowing information to flow between token positions within a patch. Together, these mechanisms let the model effectively learn intra-patch dependencies. Block-wise generation also changes the computational landscape: it shifts some of the workload from sequence length to model depth (number of flow layers), reducing the effective sequence length. As shown in Fig. 7, this can significantly lower the total FLOPs needed to process a sequence (e.g., 1024 tokens) compared to standard transformers, especially with larger patch sizes, all within a generalized autoregressive framework. We also trained a discrete autoregressive language model with a two-token prediction objective and compared its generative perplexity (measured by GPT-2 Large) to our model, conditioning on varying prefix lengths. The results show a large gap, highlighting how standard AR LMs break down when the next-single-token assumption is violated. See Fig. 5 for generative PPL with patch size 2. See Table 3 for unconditional samples from TarFlowLM .

texts.orig: Flexible vocabulary size. Akey flexibility of our model is the ability to freely choose the number of mixture components in each stacked autoregressive flow layer, especially when using mixture-based transformations (see Sections 3.2.1 and 3.2.2). This number, denoted as V in Eq. equation 5 (1D) and Eq. equation 7 ( d -dimensional), serves as an internal "vocabulary" for the flow, and can be set independently for each layer.
texts.text: Flexible vocabulary size. Akey flexibility of our model is the ability to freely choose the number of mixture components in each stacked autoregressive flow layer, especially when using mixture-based transformations (see Sections 3.2.1 and 3.2.2). This number, denoted as V in Eq. equation 5 (1D) and Eq. equation 7 ( d -dimensional), serves as an internal "vocabulary" for the flow, and can be set independently for each layer.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: This design enables strategies like coarse-to-fine modeling: earlier layers can use fewer components to capture broad structure, while later layers use more to refine details. Importantly, the internal mixture size does not need to match the data's discrete vocabulary.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Flexible text editing in continuous space. Our framework models language in a continuous latent space using stacked autoregressive normalizing flows, enabling us to decode intermediate representations at each flow layer. This provides a unique, step-by-step view of how the model incrementally edits and refines latent states into coherent text-a process that is difficult to realize in discrete token spaces. A key advantage of this approach is its natural support for coarse-to-fine generation: early flow layers make broad, global changes to the latent sequence, while later layers focus on increasingly fine-grained adjustments. We quantify this progression using several metrics, including the mean L2 norm of token embeddings, mean pairwise cosine similarity, Participation Ratio (PR), and centroid movement between layers. Typically, we observe that the mean L2 norm decreases and cosine similarity increases across layers, indicating that representations become more compact and internally coherent. The centroid movement diminishes with depth, reflecting the transition from coarse to fine editing. Alternating flow directions (e.g., left-to-right and right-to-left) further enrich the representations by integrating information from both past and future context. This hierarchical, coarse-to-fine refinement is a direct benefit of operating in a continuous space, where smooth, learnable edits are possible at every stage. Fig. 6 visualizes per-layer edits on decoded text. For further details and quantitative results, see Appendix G.
texts.text: Flexible text editing in continuous space. Our framework models language in a continuous latent space using stacked autoregressive normalizing flows, enabling us to decode intermediate representations at each flow layer. This provides a unique, step-by-step view of how the model incrementally edits and refines latent states into coherent text-a process that is difficult to realize in discrete token spaces. A key advantage of this approach is its natural support for coarse-to-fine generation: early flow layers make broad, global changes to the latent sequence, while later layers focus on increasingly fine-grained adjustments. We quantify this progression using several metrics, including the mean L2 norm of token embeddings, mean pairwise cosine similarity, Participation Ratio (PR), and centroid movement between layers. Typically, we observe that the mean L2 norm decreases and cosine similarity increases across layers, indicating that representations become more compact and internally coherent. The centroid movement diminishes with depth, reflecting the transition from coarse to fine editing. Alternating flow directions (e.g., left-to-right and right-to-left) further enrich the representations by integrating information from both past and future context. This hierarchical, coarse-to-fine refinement is a direct benefit of operating in a continuous space, where smooth, learnable edits are possible at every stage. Fig. 6 visualizes per-layer edits on decoded text. For further details and quantitative results, see Appendix G.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Model Ablation We performed ablation studies on OPENWEBTEXT to evaluate key architectural choices (see Fig. 2). Adding dimension-wise autoregressive affine flows to a base model yields only minor NELBO improvement. Replacing affine couplings with 1-D mixture-CDF transforms greatly improves NELBO, and using full d -dimensional Mixture-Rosenblatt flow further boosts performance. Progressive layer-wise training-freezing earlier flow blocks-achieves the best results, demonstrating the benefit of a simple curriculum over flow depth. We also examined the effect of the number of Gaussian components V in 1 -D mixture-CDF couplings on TEXT8 (Fig. 4). We did not use MAF module to isolate the mixture coupling effect. A single component (affine flow) gives high BPC, while all other > 1 number of mixtures can work well.
texts.text: Model Ablation We performed ablation studies on OPENWEBTEXT to evaluate key architectural choices (see Fig. 2). Adding dimension-wise autoregressive affine flows to a base model yields only minor NELBO improvement. Replacing affine couplings with 1-D mixture-CDF transforms greatly improves NELBO, and using full d -dimensional Mixture-Rosenblatt flow further boosts performance. Progressive layer-wise training-freezing earlier flow blocks-achieves the best results, demonstrating the benefit of a simple curriculum over flow depth. We also examined the effect of the number of Gaussian components V in 1 -D mixture-CDF couplings on TEXT8 (Fig. 4). We did not use MAF module to isolate the mixture coupling effect. A single component (affine flow) gives high BPC, while all other > 1 number of mixtures can work well.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 5 Conclusion
texts.text: 5 Conclusion

texts.label: text
texts.orig: This work introduced TarFlowLM , a novel framework that recasts language modeling in continuous latent spaces using Transformer-based autoregressive normalizing flows. Our approach achieves strong likelihood performance while enabling significant flexibility, including bi-directional context, block-wise generation, and hierarchical refinement. These findings highlight the potential of continuous latent variable models with mixture-based transformations to advance flexible and expressive sequence generation.
texts.text: This work introduced TarFlowLM , a novel framework that recasts language modeling in continuous latent spaces using Transformer-based autoregressive normalizing flows. Our approach achieves strong likelihood performance while enabling significant flexibility, including bi-directional context, block-wise generation, and hierarchical refinement. These findings highlight the potential of continuous latent variable models with mixture-based transformations to advance flexible and expressive sequence generation.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Limitations
texts.text: Limitations

texts.label: text
texts.orig: While our framework, TarFlowLM , demonstrates strong likelihood performance and notable modeling flexibility, we acknowledge certain limitations inherent to the current instantiation, particularly concerning sampling efficiency. Autoregressive normalizing flows, by their nature, involve a sequential generation process. Each step in the generation depends on the previously generated ones, which can lead to slower sampling speeds compared to models that allow for more parallelized generation, such as some non-autoregressive or diffusion-based approaches.
texts.text: While our framework, TarFlowLM , demonstrates strong likelihood performance and notable modeling flexibility, we acknowledge certain limitations inherent to the current instantiation, particularly concerning sampling efficiency. Autoregressive normalizing flows, by their nature, involve a sequential generation process. Each step in the generation depends on the previously generated ones, which can lead to slower sampling speeds compared to models that allow for more parallelized generation, such as some non-autoregressive or diffusion-based approaches.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In this work, our primary focus was on establishing the viability of transformer-based autoregressive flows for flexible language modeling in continuous latent spaces, emphasizing likelihood estimation and the exploration of novel modeling capabilities like block-wise generation and hierarchical refinement. Consequently, a systematic investigation into optimizing sampling speed or exploring advanced sampling techniques (e.g., parallel decoding strategies, distillation) for this class of flowbased language models was beyond the scope of the current paper.
texts.text: In this work, our primary focus was on establishing the viability of transformer-based autoregressive flows for flexible language modeling in continuous latent spaces, emphasizing likelihood estimation and the exploration of novel modeling capabilities like block-wise generation and hierarchical refinement. Consequently, a systematic investigation into optimizing sampling speed or exploring advanced sampling techniques (e.g., parallel decoding strategies, distillation) for this class of flowbased language models was beyond the scope of the current paper.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Improving the sampling efficiency of autoregressive flow models for text generation remains an important and active area of research. We consider this a promising direction for future work, which could involve developing specialized architectural modifications, exploring alternative flow parametrizations, or adapting techniques from other generative modeling paradigms to accelerate the sampling process without compromising the expressive power and flexibility demonstrated in this paper.
texts.text: Improving the sampling efficiency of autoregressive flow models for text generation remains an important and active area of research. We consider this a promising direction for future work, which could involve developing specialized architectural modifications, exploring alternative flow parametrizations, or adapting techniques from other generative modeling paradigms to accelerate the sampling process without compromising the expressive power and flexibility demonstrated in this paper.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Broader Impacts
texts.text: Broader Impacts

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The research presented in this paper, TarFlowLM , introduces a novel framework for language modeling in continuous latent spaces using transformer-based autoregressive normalizing flows. As with many advancements in machine learning and artificial intelligence, particularly in the domain of generative models, this work has the potential for a range of societal impacts, both positive and negative.
texts.text: The research presented in this paper, TarFlowLM , introduces a novel framework for language modeling in continuous latent spaces using transformer-based autoregressive normalizing flows. As with many advancements in machine learning and artificial intelligence, particularly in the domain of generative models, this work has the potential for a range of societal impacts, both positive and negative.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Potential Positive Societal Impacts. The enhanced flexibility offered by our approach-such as bi-directional context modeling, block-wise generation, and hierarchical refinement-could lead to significant positive developments.
texts.text: Potential Positive Societal Impacts. The enhanced flexibility offered by our approach-such as bi-directional context modeling, block-wise generation, and hierarchical refinement-could lead to significant positive developments.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Improved Creative Tools: Models capable of more nuanced and controllable generation could serve as powerful assistive tools for writers, artists, and designers, helping to brainstorm, draft, and refine creative content. The ability to edit text in a continuous latent space and observe intermediate generation steps might offer new paradigms for human-AI collaboration in creative tasks.
texts.text: Improved Creative Tools: Models capable of more nuanced and controllable generation could serve as powerful assistive tools for writers, artists, and designers, helping to brainstorm, draft, and refine creative content. The ability to edit text in a continuous latent space and observe intermediate generation steps might offer new paradigms for human-AI collaboration in creative tasks.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Enhanced Controllability in NLP Applications: The flexible architectural components, like adaptable patch sizes and independent mixture component selection per layer, might enable finer-grained control over the generation process. This could be beneficial for applications requiring specific stylistic attributes, content constraints, or conditional generation tasks (e.g., personalized dialogue systems, summarization with specific focuses).
texts.text: Enhanced Controllability in NLP Applications: The flexible architectural components, like adaptable patch sizes and independent mixture component selection per layer, might enable finer-grained control over the generation process. This could be beneficial for applications requiring specific stylistic attributes, content constraints, or conditional generation tasks (e.g., personalized dialogue systems, summarization with specific focuses).

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Advancements in Fundamental Understanding: By exploring language modeling in continuous latent spaces, this research contributes to a broader understanding of how complex sequential data can be represented and manipulated. Such foundational insights can spur further innovation in machine learning for sequences beyond just text, potentially impacting fields like bioinformatics or time-series analysis.
texts.text: Advancements in Fundamental Understanding: By exploring language modeling in continuous latent spaces, this research contributes to a broader understanding of how complex sequential data can be represented and manipulated. Such foundational insights can spur further innovation in machine learning for sequences beyond just text, potentially impacting fields like bioinformatics or time-series analysis.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Potential for More Efficient Modeling: While sampling efficiency is noted as a limitation, the architectural flexibility (e.g., block-wise processing potentially reducing effective sequence length for attention mechanisms) hints at avenues for developing more computationally efficient models for certain tasks or sequence lengths, which could make advanced NLP capabilities more accessible.
texts.text: Potential for More Efficient Modeling: While sampling efficiency is noted as a limitation, the architectural flexibility (e.g., block-wise processing potentially reducing effective sequence length for attention mechanisms) hints at avenues for developing more computationally efficient models for certain tasks or sequence lengths, which could make advanced NLP capabilities more accessible.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Potential Negative Societal Impacts and Risks. Advancements in generative language modeling, including the techniques explored in this paper, also carry inherent risks that warrant careful consideration.
texts.text: Potential Negative Societal Impacts and Risks. Advancements in generative language modeling, including the techniques explored in this paper, also carry inherent risks that warrant careful consideration.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Economic Disruption: As generative AI tools become more capable, there is potential for economic disruption in professions that involve content creation or communication. While these tools can also augment human capabilities, the long-term societal adjustments need to be considered.
texts.text: Economic Disruption: As generative AI tools become more capable, there is potential for economic disruption in professions that involve content creation or communication. While these tools can also augment human capabilities, the long-term societal adjustments need to be considered.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Security Concerns: The potential for generating human-like text could be used in social engineering attacks, to create more convincing phishing emails, or to automate an abusive online presence.
texts.text: Security Concerns: The potential for generating human-like text could be used in social engineering attacks, to create more convincing phishing emails, or to automate an abusive online presence.

texts.orig: Considerations for Responsible Development. While this work is primarily foundational, focusing on a novel modeling paradigm, it is crucial for the research community to engage in ongoing discussions about the responsible development and deployment of such technologies. Future research building upon TarFlowLM should ideally incorporate investigations into:
texts.text: Considerations for Responsible Development. While this work is primarily foundational, focusing on a novel modeling paradigm, it is crucial for the research community to engage in ongoing discussions about the responsible development and deployment of such technologies. Future research building upon TarFlowLM should ideally incorporate investigations into:

texts.text: Methods for identifying and mitigating biases in the learned representations and generated outputs.

texts.orig: Our aim is to contribute to the open scientific exploration of generative models. We believe that by understanding the capabilities and limitations of new approaches like TarFlowLM , the community is better equipped to foresee potential impacts and work towards harnessing these technologies for societal benefit while mitigating potential harms.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: A Details for 1 -D Mixture-CDF Flow Layer
texts.text: A Details for 1 -D Mixture-CDF Flow Layer

texts.label: text
texts.orig: This section details the derivation for the 1D Mixture of Gaussians CDF (Cumulative Distribution Function) normalizing flow layer, as introduced in Section 3.2.1 of the main text. The conditional mixture density for a single scalar component z t,i given its context ( z <t , z t,<i ) is defined in Eq. equation 5 as:
texts.text: This section details the derivation for the 1D Mixture of Gaussians CDF (Cumulative Distribution Function) normalizing flow layer, as introduced in Section 3.2.1 of the main text. The conditional mixture density for a single scalar component z t,i given its context ( z <t , z t,<i ) is defined in Eq. equation 5 as:

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: p ( z t,i | z <t , z t,<i ) = V ∑ k =1 π [ k ] N ( z t,i ; m [ k ] , σ 2 [ k ])

texts.label: text
texts.orig: where the parameter bundle [ π , m , σ 2 ] (containing V mixture weights, means, and variances respectively) is predicted by a Transformer model from the context ( z <t , z t,<i ) . We denote this 1D mixture probability density function (PDF) as p mix1 ( z t,i | z <t , z t,<i ) . This conditional PDF can be used to construct an invertible transformation suitable for normalizing flows.
texts.text: where the parameter bundle [ π , m , σ 2 ] (containing V mixture weights, means, and variances respectively) is predicted by a Transformer model from the context ( z <t , z t,<i ) . We denote this 1D mixture probability density function (PDF) as p mix1 ( z t,i | z <t , z t,<i ) . This conditional PDF can be used to construct an invertible transformation suitable for normalizing flows.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The CDF of p mix1 ( z t,i | z <t , z t,<i ) , denoted F mix1 ( z t,i ; z <t , z t,<i ) , is a weighted sum of the CDFs of the individual Gaussian components:
texts.text: The CDF of p mix1 ( z t,i | z <t , z t,<i ) , denoted F mix1 ( z t,i ; z <t , z t,<i ) , is a weighted sum of the CDFs of the individual Gaussian components:

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: F mix1 ( z t,i ; z <t , z t,<i ) = V ∑ k =1 π [ k ]Φ ( z t,i -m [ k ] σ [ k ] )

texts.label: text
texts.orig: where Φ( · ) is the CDF of the standard normal distribution N (0 , 1) , and σ [ k ] = √ σ 2 [ k ] is the standard deviation of the k -th component. The mixture parameters π [ k ] , m [ k ] , σ [ k ] are functions of ( z <t , z t,<i ) .
texts.text: where Φ( · ) is the CDF of the standard normal distribution N (0 , 1) , and σ [ k ] = √ σ 2 [ k ] is the standard deviation of the k -th component. The mixture parameters π [ k ] , m [ k ] , σ [ k ] are functions of ( z <t , z t,<i ) .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Using the probability integral transform, if a random variable Z t,i is drawn from p mix1 ( Z t,i | z <t , z t,<i ) , then F mix1 ( Z t,i ; z <t , z t,<i ) is uniformly distributed on [0 , 1] . To map z t,i to a variable u t,i that follows a standard normal distribution N (0 , 1) , we compose the mixture CDF with the inverse standard normal CDF, Φ -1 ( · ) :
texts.text: Using the probability integral transform, if a random variable Z t,i is drawn from p mix1 ( Z t,i | z <t , z t,<i ) , then F mix1 ( Z t,i ; z <t , z t,<i ) is uniformly distributed on [0 , 1] . To map z t,i to a variable u t,i that follows a standard normal distribution N (0 , 1) , we compose the mixture CDF with the inverse standard normal CDF, Φ -1 ( · ) :

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: u t,i = Φ -1 ( F mix1 ( z t,i ; z <t , z t,<i )) (10)

texts.label: text
texts.orig: This transformation is invertible. The variable z t,i can be recovered from u t,i using z t,i = F -1 mix1 (Φ( u t,i ); z <t , z t,<i ) , where F -1 mix1 is the inverse CDF (quantile function) of the mixture. F -1 mix1 generally does not have a closed-form expression and may require numerical methods for its evaluation.
texts.text: This transformation is invertible. The variable z t,i can be recovered from u t,i using z t,i = F -1 mix1 (Φ( u t,i ); z <t , z t,<i ) , where F -1 mix1 is the inverse CDF (quantile function) of the mixture. F -1 mix1 generally does not have a closed-form expression and may require numerical methods for its evaluation.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: For this transformation to serve as a normalizing flow layer, its Jacobian determinant is needed. In this 1D case, this is the absolute value of the derivative ∂u t,i ∂z t,i . We compute this using the chain rule. Let y cdf = F mix1 ( z t,i ; z <t , z t,<i ) . Then u t,i = Φ -1 ( y cdf ) , and
texts.text: For this transformation to serve as a normalizing flow layer, its Jacobian determinant is needed. In this 1D case, this is the absolute value of the derivative ∂u t,i ∂z t,i . We compute this using the chain rule. Let y cdf = F mix1 ( z t,i ; z <t , z t,<i ) . Then u t,i = Φ -1 ( y cdf ) , and

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: ∂u t,i ∂z t,i = d Φ -1 ( y cdf ) dy cdf ∣ ∣ ∣ ∣ ∣ y cdf = F mix1 ( z t,i ; z <t , z t,<i ) · ∂F mix1 ( z t,i ; z <t , z t,<i ) ∂z t,i

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We evaluate each term:
texts.text: We evaluate each term:

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 1. The derivative of the inverse standard normal CDF, d Φ -1 ( y cdf ) dy cdf : If u t,i = Φ -1 ( y cdf ) , then y cdf = Φ( u t,i ) . Differentiating y cdf = Φ( u t,i ) with respect to u t,i yields dy cdf du t,i = N ( u t,i ; 0 , 1) , where N ( u t,i ; 0 , 1) is the PDF of the standard normal distribution. Therefore, du t,i dy cdf = d Φ -1 ( y cdf ) dy cdf = 1 N ( u t,i ;0 , 1) .
texts.text: The derivative of the inverse standard normal CDF, d Φ -1 ( y cdf ) dy cdf : If u t,i = Φ -1 ( y cdf ) , then y cdf = Φ( u t,i ) . Differentiating y cdf = Φ( u t,i ) with respect to u t,i yields dy cdf du t,i = N ( u t,i ; 0 , 1) , where N ( u t,i ; 0 , 1) is the PDF of the standard normal distribution. Therefore, du t,i dy cdf = d Φ -1 ( y cdf ) dy cdf = 1 N ( u t,i ;0 , 1) .

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 2. The derivative of the mixture CDF with respect to z t,i , ∂F mix1 ( z t,i ; z <t , z t,<i ) ∂z t,i :
texts.text: The derivative of the mixture CDF with respect to z t,i , ∂F mix1 ( z t,i ; z <t , z t,<i ) ∂z t,i :

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: ∂F mix1 ( z t,i ; z <t , z t,<i ) ∂z t,i = ∂ ∂z t,i V ∑ k =1 π [ k ]Φ ( z t,i -m [ k ] σ [ k ] ) = V ∑ k =1 π [ k ] N ( z t,i -m [ k ] σ [ k ] ; 0 , 1 ) · 1 σ [ k ]

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Recognizing that 1 σ [ k ] N ( z t,i -m [ k ] σ [ k ] ; 0 , 1 ) is the PDF N ( z t,i ; m [ k ] , σ 2 [ k ]) , the sum becomes:
texts.text: Recognizing that 1 σ [ k ] N ( z t,i -m [ k ] σ [ k ] ; 0 , 1 ) is the PDF N ( z t,i ; m [ k ] , σ 2 [ k ]) , the sum becomes:

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: ∂F mix1 ( z t,i ; z <t , z t,<i ) ∂z t,i = V ∑ k =1 π [ k ] N ( z t,i ; m [ k ] , σ 2 [ k ]) = p mix1 ( z t,i | z <t , z t,<i )

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Thus, the derivative of the mixture CDF is its PDF.
texts.text: Thus, the derivative of the mixture CDF is its PDF.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Combining these results, the derivative of the transformation is:
texts.text: Combining these results, the derivative of the transformation is:

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: ∂u t,i ∂z t,i = p mix1 ( z t,i | z <t , z t,<i ) N ( u t,i ; 0 , 1)

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The log absolute Jacobian determinant is therefore:
texts.text: The log absolute Jacobian determinant is therefore:

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: log ∣ ∣ ∣ ∣ ∂u t,i ∂z t,i ∣ ∣ ∣ ∣ = log p mix1 ( z t,i | z <t , z t,<i ) -log N ( u t,i ; 0 , 1) (11)

texts.label: text
texts.orig: This equation relates the log-density of z t,i under the mixture model p mix1 to the log-density of u t,i under the standard normal base distribution and the log-Jacobian term. Rearranging Eq. equation 11 according to the change of variables formula ( log p ( z ) = log p ( u ) + log | det J f | ):
texts.text: This equation relates the log-density of z t,i under the mixture model p mix1 to the log-density of u t,i under the standard normal base distribution and the log-Jacobian term. Rearranging Eq. equation 11 according to the change of variables formula ( log p ( z ) = log p ( u ) + log | det J f | ):

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: log p mix1 ( z t,i | z <t , z t,<i ) = log N ( u t,i ; 0 , 1) + log ∣ ∣ ∣ ∣ ∂u t,i ∂z t,i ∣ ∣ ∣ ∣

texts.label: text
texts.orig: This confirms that learning the mixture parameters [ π , m , σ 2 ] is equivalent to learning this 1D Mixture-CDF flow layer.
texts.text: This confirms that learning the mixture parameters [ π , m , σ 2 ] is equivalent to learning this 1D Mixture-CDF flow layer.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: When such a transformation is used as a layer ℓ in a stack of flows, z t,i corresponds to the i -th scalar component of the input to this layer, h ( ℓ -1) t,i . The output u t,i corresponds to the i -th scalar component h ( ℓ ) t,i . The parameters for the mixture density p mix1 ( h ( ℓ -1) t,i | cond ( ℓ ) t,i ) for layer ℓ are determined by a neural network conditioned on cond ( ℓ ) t,i , which is the layer-specific conditioning information. The term log p mix1 ( h ( ℓ -1) t,i | cond ( ℓ ) t,i ) in the log-determinant calculation (Eq. equation 11, adapted for layer ℓ ) represents the log-probability of the layer's input under the specific 1D mixture model implemented by that layer.
texts.text: When such a transformation is used as a layer ℓ in a stack of flows, z t,i corresponds to the i -th scalar component of the input to this layer, h ( ℓ -1) t,i . The output u t,i corresponds to the i -th scalar component h ( ℓ ) t,i . The parameters for the mixture density p mix1 ( h ( ℓ -1) t,i | cond ( ℓ ) t,i ) for layer ℓ are determined by a neural network conditioned on cond ( ℓ ) t,i , which is the layer-specific conditioning information. The term log p mix1 ( h ( ℓ -1) t,i | cond ( ℓ ) t,i ) in the log-determinant calculation (Eq. equation 11, adapted for layer ℓ ) represents the log-probability of the layer's input under the specific 1D mixture model implemented by that layer.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: A.1 Proof of Proposition 1
texts.text: A.1 Proof of Proposition 1

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Proposition 1. Let p mix -1 ( z ) = ∑ V k =1 π k N ( z ; m k , σ 2 k ) be a 1D MoG probability density function (PDF), and let F mix -1 ( z ) be its corresponding cumulative distribution function (CDF). The transformation f : R → R defined by u = f ( z ) = Φ -1 ( F mix -1 ( z )) , where Φ is the standard normal CDF, is an exact normalizing flow between the density p mix -1 ( z ) and the standard normal density N ( u ; 0 , 1) .
texts.text: Proposition 1. Let p mix -1 ( z ) = ∑ V k =1 π k N ( z ; m k , σ 2 k ) be a 1D MoG probability density function (PDF), and let F mix -1 ( z ) be its corresponding cumulative distribution function (CDF). The transformation f : R → R defined by u = f ( z ) = Φ -1 ( F mix -1 ( z )) , where Φ is the standard normal CDF, is an exact normalizing flow between the density p mix -1 ( z ) and the standard normal density N ( u ; 0 , 1) .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Proof. The proof demonstrates the equivalence from both forward and inverse directions. A key property is that F mix -1 ( z ) is strictly increasing, since its derivative, F ′ mix -1 ( z ) = p mix -1 ( z ) , is a sum of strictly positive Gaussian densities and is therefore strictly positive. Thus, F mix -1 is a bijection from R to (0 , 1) .
texts.text: Proof. The proof demonstrates the equivalence from both forward and inverse directions. A key property is that F mix -1 ( z ) is strictly increasing, since its derivative, F ′ mix -1 ( z ) = p mix -1 ( z ) , is a sum of strictly positive Gaussian densities and is therefore strictly positive. Thus, F mix -1 is a bijection from R to (0 , 1) .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Forward Direction ( Z → U ). Assume a random variable Z ∼ p mix -1 ( z ) . We show that the transformed variable U = f ( Z ) follows a standard normal distribution by computing its CDF:

texts.orig: B Details for d -D Mixture-Rosenblatt Flow Layer
texts.text: B Details for d -D Mixture-Rosenblatt Flow Layer

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We use C to denote the conditioning context variable. A d -dimensional conditional mixture density, such as p mix ( z |C ) = ∑ V k =1 π k ( C ) N ( z ; m k ( C ) , s 2 k ( C ) I d ) , can be realized as an exact and invertible normalizing flow layer. This construction is used for modeling complex conditional distributions within the flow framework. For example, it can represent the prior's conditional distribution p ( z t | z <t ) as defined in Eq. equation 7, or a general conditional density p ( ℓ ) mix ,t ( h ( ℓ -1) t | cond ( ℓ ) t ) for an intermediate layer ℓ in a stack of flows (see Section 3.3).
texts.text: We use C to denote the conditioning context variable. A d -dimensional conditional mixture density, such as p mix ( z |C ) = ∑ V k =1 π k ( C ) N ( z ; m k ( C ) , s 2 k ( C ) I d ) , can be realized as an exact and invertible normalizing flow layer. This construction is used for modeling complex conditional distributions within the flow framework. For example, it can represent the prior's conditional distribution p ( z t | z <t ) as defined in Eq. equation 7, or a general conditional density p ( ℓ ) mix ,t ( h ( ℓ -1) t | cond ( ℓ ) t ) for an intermediate layer ℓ in a stack of flows (see Section 3.3).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In this description, z ∈ R d denotes the input variable to this flow layer. If this layer is the first transformation applied to a latent variable z t from the VAE, then z corresponds to z t (which is also h (0) t in the notation of stacked flows). If it is an intermediate layer ℓ in the stack, z corresponds to h ( ℓ -1) t . The term C represents the conditioning information, such as z <t for the prior, or a layer-specific context cond ( ℓ ) t . The parameters m k and s 2 k of the Gaussian components can either be predicted based on C , or, as in Eq. equation 7, they can be part of a shared codebook (e.g., { µ k , σ 2 k } V k =1 ) where only the mixture weights π k ( C ) are predicted.
texts.text: In this description, z ∈ R d denotes the input variable to this flow layer. If this layer is the first transformation applied to a latent variable z t from the VAE, then z corresponds to z t (which is also h (0) t in the notation of stacked flows). If it is an intermediate layer ℓ in the stack, z corresponds to h ( ℓ -1) t . The term C represents the conditioning information, such as z <t for the prior, or a layer-specific context cond ( ℓ ) t . The parameters m k and s 2 k of the Gaussian components can either be predicted based on C , or, as in Eq. equation 7, they can be part of a shared codebook (e.g., { µ k , σ 2 k } V k =1 ) where only the mixture weights π k ( C ) are predicted.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The goal is to define an invertible transformation g d : R d → R d , denoted u = g d ( z ; C ) , such that the output variable u ∈ R d follows a standard d -dimensional Gaussian distribution, i.e., u ∼ N ( 0 , I d ) . This transformation is constructed by processing the components of z = ( z 1 , . . . , z d ) sequentially, from i = 1 to d , using a method related to the Rosenblatt transformation. The forward pass is detailed in Algorithm 1.
texts.text: The goal is to define an invertible transformation g d : R d → R d , denoted u = g d ( z ; C ) , such that the output variable u ∈ R d follows a standard d -dimensional Gaussian distribution, i.e., u ∼ N ( 0 , I d ) . This transformation is constructed by processing the components of z = ( z 1 , . . . , z d ) sequentially, from i = 1 to d , using a method related to the Rosenblatt transformation. The forward pass is detailed in Algorithm 1.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Forward Transformation z ↦→ u : The transformation from z to u is built sequentially, processing one component at a time. At each step i , u i is computed from z i using C and the previously processed components z <i = ( z 1 , . . . , z i -1 ) . Mixture component weights are maintained and updated throughout this process.
texts.text: Forward Transformation z ↦→ u : The transformation from z to u is built sequentially, processing one component at a time. At each step i , u i is computed from z i using C and the previously processed components z <i = ( z 1 , . . . , z i -1 ) . Mixture component weights are maintained and updated throughout this process.

texts.orig: Jacobian Determinant : The Jacobian matrix J g d ( z ) of the forward transformation u = g d ( z ; C ) has entries ( J g d ) ℓi = ∂u ℓ ∂z i . Due to the sequential construction, where u ℓ depends only on z 1 , . . . , z ℓ (and C ), the Jacobian matrix is lower-triangular. Its determinant is the product of its diagonal entries: det J g d ( z ) = ∏ d i =1 ∂u i ∂z i .
texts.text: Jacobian Determinant : The Jacobian matrix J g d ( z ) of the forward transformation u = g d ( z ; C ) has entries ( J g d ) ℓi = ∂u ℓ ∂z i . Its determinant is the product of its diagonal entries: det J g d ( z ) = ∏ d i =1 ∂u i ∂z i .

texts.orig: B.1 Proof of Proposition 2
texts.text: B.1 Proof of Proposition 2

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Here we provide formal proofs for the normalizing flow layers based on the Mixture of Gaussians (MoG) CDF, as described in Appendices A and B. We establish that these transformations are exact, invertible, and correctly model the target mixture densities.
texts.text: Here we provide formal proofs for the normalizing flow layers based on the Mixture of Gaussians (MoG) CDF, as described in Appendices A and B. We establish that these transformations are exact, invertible, and correctly model the target mixture densities.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The core of the d -dimensional Mixture-Rosenblatt flow lies in sequentially computing the true conditional probability distribution p ( z i | z <i ) for each dimension. The following lemma formally derives this distribution and shows that it takes the form of a 1D Mixture of Gaussians, which is analytically tractable.
texts.text: The core of the d -dimensional Mixture-Rosenblatt flow lies in sequentially computing the true conditional probability distribution p ( z i | z <i ) for each dimension. The following lemma formally derives this distribution and shows that it takes the form of a 1D Mixture of Gaussians, which is analytically tractable.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Lemma 1 (Conditional Distribution of an Isotropic MoG) . Let z ∈ R d be a random vector whose probability density function is a mixture of V isotropic Gaussians:
texts.text: Lemma 1 (Conditional Distribution of an Isotropic MoG) . Let z ∈ R d be a random vector whose probability density function is a mixture of V isotropic Gaussians:

texts.text: Proof. We derive the conditional distribution p ( z i | z <i ) by introducing a latent categorical variable K ∈ { 1 , . . . , V } that indicates which Gaussian component z is drawn from. The hierarchical model is:

texts.label: text
texts.orig: 1. The Component Density: p ( z i | z <i , K = k ) . This term is the conditional density of z i given z <i , under the condition that we know the sample originates from component k . For a given component k , z follows a single multivariate Gaussian distribution N ( m k , σ 2 k I d ) . The covariance matrix σ 2 k I d is diagonal, which implies that all dimensions of z are mutually independent *when conditioned on K = k *. Therefore, the value of z i is independent of the preceding values z <i :
texts.text: 1. The Component Density: p ( z i | z <i , K = k ) . This term is the conditional density of z i given z <i , under the condition that we know the sample originates from component k . For a given component k , z follows a single multivariate Gaussian distribution N ( m k , σ 2 k I d ) . The covariance matrix σ 2 k I d is diagonal, which implies that all dimensions of z are mutually independent *when conditioned on K = k *. Therefore, the value of z i is independent of the preceding values z <i :

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: p ( z i | z <i , K = k ) = p ( z i | K = k ) .

texts.text: Proposition 2. Let p mix ( z ) = ∑ V k =1 π k N ( z ; m k , σ 2 k I d ) be a d -dimensional isotropic MoG PDF. Let the transformation g : R d → R d be defined by the sequential application for i = 1 , . . . , d :

texts.text: where F i ( z i | z <i ) is the cumulative distribution function (CDF) of the 1D MoG conditional density p ( z i | z <i ) as derived in Lemma 1. This transformation is an exact normalizing flow between p mix ( z ) and the standard d -dimensional normal distribution N ( u ; 0 , I d ) .

texts.text: Proof. The proof relies on the chain rule of probability, p mix ( z ) = ∏ d i =1 p ( z i | z <i ) , and the result of Lemma 1.

texts.orig: Forward Direction ( Z → U ). Assume a random vector Z ∼ p mix ( z ) . We show by induction that the components of the transformed vector U = g ( Z ) are independent and identically distributed as N (0 , 1) . By Lemma 1, F i ( Z i | Z <i ) is the true conditional CDF of Z i . By the conditional probability integral transform, the random variable W i = F i ( Z i | Z <i ) is uniformly distributed on [0 , 1] and is independent of the conditioning random vector Z <i . Consequently, U i = Φ -1 ( W i ) is distributed as N (0 , 1) and is also independent of Z <i . Since ( U 1 , . . . , U i -1 ) is an invertible function of Z <i , U i is also independent of ( U 1 , . . . , U i -1 ) . By induction, all components of U are i.i.d. N (0 , 1) , so U ∼ N ( 0 , I d ) .
texts.text: Forward Direction ( Z → U ). Assume a random vector Z ∼ p mix ( z ) . We show by induction that the components of the transformed vector U = g ( Z ) are independent and identically distributed as N (0 , 1) . By Lemma 1, F i ( Z i | Z <i ) is the true conditional CDF of Z i . By the conditional probability integral transform, the random variable W i = F i ( Z i | Z <i ) is uniformly distributed on [0 , 1] and is independent of the conditioning random vector Z <i . Consequently, U i = Φ -1 ( W i ) is distributed as N (0 , 1) and is also independent of Z <i . Since ( U 1 , . . . , U i -1 ) is an invertible function of Z <i , U i is also independent of ( U 1 , . . . , U i -1 ) . By induction, all components of U are i.i.d. N (0 , 1) , so U ∼ N ( 0 , I d ) .

texts.orig: Inverse Direction ( U → Z ). Assume a random vector U ∼ N ( 0 , I d ) . The inverse transformation Z = g -1 ( U ) is computed by sequentially solving F i ( Z i | Z <i ) = Φ( U i ) for Z i . This procedure is equivalent to ancestral sampling from the factorized distribution: for each i = 1 , . . . , d , one samples Z i from the true conditional distribution p ( z i | Z <i ) via inverse transform sampling. The joint density of the resulting vector Z is therefore ∏ d i =1 p ( z i | z <i ) = p mix ( z ) .
texts.text: Inverse Direction ( U → Z ). Assume a random vector U ∼ N ( 0 , I d ) . The inverse transformation Z = g -1 ( U ) is computed by sequentially solving F i ( Z i | Z <i ) = Φ( U i ) for Z i . This procedure is equivalent to ancestral sampling from the factorized distribution: for each i = 1 , . . . , d , one samples Z i from the true conditional distribution p ( z i | Z <i ) via inverse transform sampling. The joint density of the resulting vector Z is therefore ∏ d i =1 p ( z i | z <i ) = p mix ( z ) .
